title: The Culture of Mammalian Cells on Nanostructured Silicon
abstract:
Communications
318
Ó WILEY-VCH Verlag GmbH, D-69469 Weinheim, 1999
0935-9648/99/0403-0318 $ 17.50+.50/0
Adv. Mater. 1999, 11, No. 4
RSiCl3: Freshly prepared wafers were placed into a solution of the appro-
priate trichlorosilane (5 mM) in freshly distilled toluene for 2 h. Upon re-
moval from the toluene solution, the wafers were rinsed with chloroform
and absolute ethanol. The wafers were then baked on a hot plate at 120 C
for 2 min.
Monolayer Characterization: Contact angles were determined on a Krüss
Model G10 goniometer at room temperature and ambient relative humidity
using the sessile drop method [21]. Monolayer thickness was determined
using a Gaertner L116A ellipsometer equipped with a 632.8 nm He-Ne la-
ser. Monolayer thicknesses were determined assuming nf = 1.5, and with the
incident and reflected light making a 70 C angle to the surface normal.
Data presented are averages of at least ten different measurements on three
different areas of the wafer. Film roughness was determined in contact
mode AFM and calculated using PSI ProScan software.
Received: August 31, 1998
Final version: October 26, 1998

The Culture of Mammalian Cells on
Nanostructured Silicon
By Sue C. Bayliss, Robert Heald, D. Ian Fletcher, and
Lorraine D. Buckberry*
Devices that employ silicon-based semiconductors have
been available for in vitro biosensing applications for a
number of years. To date, however, the bioincompatibility
of this type of silicon has prevented its use in vivo.[1±5] Thus
silicon-based integrated circuits require packaging in a bio-
compatible manner if they are to be used in and interfaced
to living tissues. Nanostructured porous silicon (PSi), how-
ever, has properties that make it a very promising bioma-
terial, in particular for biologically interfaced sensing appli-
cations. Canham reported the growth of hydroxyapatite on
porous silicon, implying the possible bone implantability of
the material,[6] and our group further reported the ability
of Chinese hamster ovary cells (CHO) to adhere to PSi.[7]
The ability to culture mammalian cells directly onto PSi
offers exciting possibilities for the future of biologically in-
terfaced sensing. This could involve the development of
biologically interfaced neural networks or electronic sen-
sing with signal information being directly transduced from
a living system to a PSi device. Furthermore, PSi demon-
strates optical and optoelectronic emission and detection
properties, thus allowing it to be linked to the data logger
by optical fibers, removing the risk of electromagnetic
forces influencing the cellular response. This material could
thus facilitate the realization of devices to replace damaged
tissues in the ear, eye, skin, or nasal cavity. Such devices
could be used as sensors for receiving optical information,
which could then be converted to a biological signal and in-
terfaced to neural tissue to replace damaged eyes. Alterna-
tively, it could be employed in environmental or pharma-
ceutical
sensing
systems
by
optically
addressing
the
material and sensing wavelength shifts with a change in in-
terfaced cell viability.
This paper describes the development of cell culture sys-
tems towards a PSi:cell interface, including the deposition
of hydroxyapatite onto various silicon substrates to mea-
sure the biocompatibility of the surface, establishment of
the effect of any preculture treatments of the material in
terms of surface chemistry, and an assessment of the viabili-
ty of immortalized cells when cultured on a silicon cue sur-
_______________________
±
[*]
Dr. L. D. Buckberry
Department of Chemistry
Faculty of Applied Sciences, De Montfort University
The Gateway, Leicester LE1 9BH (UK)
R. Heald
Biomolecular Science Laboratory
Faculty of Applied Sciences, De Montfort University
The Gateway, Leicester LE1 9BH (UK)
Prof. S. C. Bayliss, D. I. Fletcher
Solid State Research Centre
Faculty of Applied Sciences, De Montfort University
The Gateway, Leicester LE1 9BH (UK)
 face to determine the feasibility of constructing biologically
interfaced sensing devices for long-term use.
The behavior of prepared semiconductor substrates on
exposure to simulated plasma consisting of physiological
levels of inorganic salts and/or the protein albumin has
been observed and characterized.[6±8] Here we report the
development of a more ªnature identicalº simulated plas-
ma (SP). Initially reported formulations were buffered
using the organic buffering agent trihydroxymethyl-amino-
methane (Tris) and hydrochloric acid to pH 7.3. However,
biological systems are buffered by a carbonate system
mediated by the enzyme carbonic anhydrase. The amount
of sodium chloride present in the SP was decreased and the
concentration of sodium carbonate was increased. This re-
sulted in the chloride levels more closely resembling phy-
siological levels while allowing the pH of the system to be
buffered at pH 7.4 using sodium carbonate (Table 1). Tris
is also a collator of alkali and alkaline earth metals such as
calcium. Hence the replacement of Tris with carbonate
buffer increases the solubility product for the components
of hydroxyapatite in the solution. In vivo up to 40 % of the
available calcium is bound to protein and therefore the ad-
dition of serum albumin (4 g/dL) would mimic the role of
ion-binding proteins in vivo. The SP was prepared by dis-
solving the inorganic components in an aqueous solution of
albumin. Adding the inorganic components before the pro-
tein caused calcium phosphate to precipitate immediately.
Test silicon substrates were prepared by the anodization of
(100) p-type silicon wafers (2±8 W). The electrochemical
etch proceeded for 15 min at 25 mA/cm2, 50 V, to produce
a nanoporous silicon surface. Other test materials used
were bulk crystalline silicon (BSi) and polycrystalline sili-
con (poly-Si) grown by plasma-enhanced chemical vapor
deposition onto bulk silicon wafers. The silicon materials
and a glass cover slip (control) were immersed for various
periods of time in the SP. The plate was then sealed to
avoid carbon dioxide from the atmosphere changing the
pH. After five days, finely divided inorganic crystallites be-
gan to form on the silicon materials. Spheroidal structures
were observed following exposure for 10 and 15 days. After
25 days
highly
structured
ªpetal-likeº
structures
had
formed, which were coated with a layer of protein after
30 days (Fig. 1). The inorganic deposits comprised calcium,
phosphate, and oxygen as determined by energy dispersive
X-ray (EDX) analysis, which indicates hydroxyapatite. On
the glass control, protein was deposited but the formation
of crystallites was not observed.
Fig. 1. SEM images of crystallites on nanoporous silicon exposed to simu-
lated plasma for a) 25 and b) 30 days. The scale bars represent 10 mm.
It is known that changes in surface chemistry and topol-
ogy affect the adherence of cells to substrate surfaces.[9] It
was predicted that the deposition of hydroxyapatite before
cell adherence would have a significant effect on both the
behavior of the cells and the ability to electrically address
materials interfaced with them. However, the formation of
hydroxyapatite crystallites on silicon materials submerged
in tissue culture media (Dulbecco's Minimum Essential
Medium [DMEM] with 1 % glutamine, 10 % fetal calf ser-
um, and 1 % penicillin/streptomycin) was not observed,
probably due to the presence of inhibitory compounds
present in the fetal bovine serum. Even if some deposition
were observed this would be expected to occur over a long-
er time span than is required for the adherence of mamma-
lian cells (days vs. hours).
To develop a model cell culture system for the study of
the cell:substrate interface, the effect of the whole culture

the cell:substrate interface, the effect of the whole culture

process on the substrates must be determined. Aseptic con-
siderations must be observed throughout the culture pro-
cess and substrates must be sterilized in fulfillment of that
requirement. The effects of sterilization by immersion in
ethanol, flaming, or autoclaving on the local chemistry
were examined using near-edge X-ray absorption fine
structure (NEXAFS) spectroscopy, scanning electron mi-
croscopy (SEM), and scanning probe microscopy (SPM),[10]
the main observations being that ethanol causes almost no
change, flaming produces a silicon oxide environment,
whilst autoclaving gives rise to increased silicon oxide and
Cell culture using silicon substrates was carried out using
materials prepared as described above. The cells were B50
(rat neuronal tumor) or CHO (Chinese hamster ovary),
and were seeded at a density of 2.5 ´ 105 cells/mL media
into six-well plates containing an inserted test material of
approximately 1 cm2. A glass cover slip was used as a con-
trol growth support material. The plates were then incu-
bated at 37 C in a 5 % CO2 : 95 % air atmosphere, in
The viability of the cells in terms of membrane integrity
and respiratory capacity was determined using the neutral
red (NR) and 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazolyl-2)-2,5-diphenyltet-
razolium bromide (MTT) assay methods of Buckberry et
al.,[11] with the following adaptation: before dye release the
test materials were washed twice in phosphate buffered sa-
line (PBS) at 37 C and transferred to a new well plate. The
relative viable cell ratio (RVCR) for each test material was
then calculated as the absorbance of the dye from the cells
grown on glass relative to that obtained from the cells
grown on test material. Values were corrected for any dif-
ference in surface area and NR uptake or MTT-formazan
formation due to the silicon surface alone. Cells were fixed
before SEM in an aqueous solution of glutaraldehyde
(25 % in PBS) containing calcium chloride (0.9 %).
The optimum substrate for the B50 cells was PSi (140 ±
4.0 %) followed by BSi (74 ± 2 %) and poly-Si (70 ± 5 %)
using the NR assay. Poly-Si (135 ± 1 %) was the optimum
substrate for CHO cells, followed by BSi and PSi (110 ±
1 %, 123 ± 8 %, respectively) using the NR assay. The rank
order was consistent with that obtained for the MTT assay
(Table 2). The morphology of the cells was stellate in na-
ture as examined by SEM following glutaraldehyde fixa-
tion. Here we have demonstrated that it is possible to cul-
ture cells onto a silicon substrate and that these cells are
viable in terms of respiratory and membrane viability.
Hence, over the period of culture (4 days) the wafers were
not fatally toxic to the cells and presented an acceptable
surface for growth. While there have been many studies re-
garding the possible toxicity of implanted silicone, there
are few positive reports on the toxicity of silicon. These
preliminary studies demonstrate a lack of toxic effect under
the conditions of culture. Also, silicic acid Si(OH)4 (postu-
lated to be formed as a result of the release of silane into
solution) is less toxic than selenious acid towards macro-
phages and fibroblasts in vitro.[3] Furthermore, cultured
keratinocytes seeded on a silicon±collagen matrix sheet
have been successfully used to cover burn wounds on nude
mice.[4] If there is a lack of toxicity then nanoporous silicon
will offer a distinct advantage over other semiconductors
such as gallium arsenide and indium arsenide, as arsenic
and indium induce apoptosis in rat thymocytes in vitro.[1]
Silicon carbides and silicon nitrides have been shown to be
toxic to V79 cells in vitro when applied in whisker or pow-
der form.[2] Here we have demonstrated that cells seeded
into a well plate containing silicon substrates grow to con-
fluence on the well plastic that surrounds the material in
the predicted time for the seeding density and incubation
conditions used. Hence, we predict that the effect of any
substances released from the silicon substrates was mini-
mal. However, it is accepted that the initial indications
from this preliminary study need to be clarified and quanti-
fied, and further studies are now in progress to confirm the
non-toxicity of these silicon substrates.
Table 2. Relative viable cell ratios for B50 and CHO cells using the MTTand
NR assays (number of samples tested n = 3 interbatch, n = 3 intrabatch).
In conclusion, we have optimized the culture of two cell
lines onto silicon materials. The optimum method for sub-
strate sterilization was shown to be autoclaving and the ex-
posure of the silicon materials to cell culture media did not
cause hydroxyapatite crystallite formation. It was further
demonstrated that immortalized mammalian cell lines can
be cultured onto a silicon substrate. The novel application
of two viability assays proved the viability of the adhered
cells over the exposure time. Over the exposure time the
silicon substrates are not toxic to the cells, which remain vi-
able in terms of respiration and cell membrane integrity.
Both of the cell lines tested grew on all three silicon sub-
strates, although CHO cells preferred the poly-Si surface
and B50 cells preferred the PSi surface (as determined and
quantified using viability assays). This variability is consis-
tent with the findings reviewed by Curtis et al.[9] Thus,
these results suggest that nanoporous silicon is a good can-
didate material for the development of optoelectronic
biointerfaced devices. To this end we are carrying out

Low Surface Energy Coatings from Waterborne
Nano-Dispersions of Polymer Complexes
By Andreas F. Thünemann,* Antje Lieske, and Bernd-
Fluorocarbon materials are unique in their ability to
repel both oil and water. There has been great develop-
mental work optimizing fluorocarbon polymers as ultra
low-adhesive materials since the discovery of poly(tetra-
fluoroethylene)[1] over fifty years ago. Commercially avail-
able fluorinated side-chain acrylic and methacrylic poly-
mers are typical low surface energy coating materials.[2]
Amongst the numerous molecular structures investigated,
a close-packed uniform CF3 surface was found to possess
the lowest surface tension ever measured (6 mN/m).[3] A
polymeric material that can maintain a stable, uniform CF3
surface may play a key role in producing non-adhesive ma-
terials with surface energies much lower than that of poly-
(tetrafluoroethylene) (20 mN/m). Applications of such a
material are manifold, ranging from protection from graf-
fiti[4] to coatings with anti-fouling properties.[5] Promising
strategies for realizing surfaces that are highly enriched
with CF3 groups include the development of self-organizing
materials such as Langmuir-Blodgett monolayers,[6,7] fluori-
nated block copolymers,[8,9] semifluorinated side-chain
ionenes,[10] fluorinated poly(a,L-glutamate)s,[11] and solid
complexes of polyelectrolytes with fluorinated surfac-
tants.[12,13] Of these promising new low surface energy
materials, the one that successfully combines low cost and
ecologically friendly synthesis has the best chance of being
introduced for daily use. A key factor here is avoidance of
organic solvents. In earlier articles we showed that fluori-
nated polyelectrolyte surfactant complexes could be pre-
pared from aqueous solution.[12,13] However, to date, coat-
ings of these materials are prepared from organic solutions.
In this paper we report a waterborne nano-dispersion of a
new complex which can be applied to smooth surfaces, re-
sulting in thin films with surface energies significantly be-
low that of poly(tetrafluoroethylene). Imperatively, the op-
tical appearance of treated surfaces is not affected by such
For complexation we used two commercially available
compounds: Poly(ethyleneimine) (PEI), a branched, water-
soluble polymer that is widely used in the paper industry.[14]
At low pH, PEI has the highest known charge density of all
polyelectrolytes. The second compound, Fluowet SB, is a
surfactant made from sulfosuccinic acid, which is esterified
with fluorinated side chains. Fluowet SB is regularly used
as a spreading agent.[15] The complexation of PEI with
Fluowet SB (stoichiometric complex with respect to
charge) was performed in water by adding solutions of PEI
to a dilute micellar solution of Fluowet SB. In contrast to
the procedure described earlier,[12,13] macroscopic precipi-
tation of the complex was avoided by using an excess of
Fluowet SB to aid dispersion. The diameters of the dis-
persed complex particles were about 200 nm, as deter-
mined by dynamic light scattering. A dilute (0.3 % w/w)
dispersion of PEI-Fluowet SB was deposited on smooth,
but chemically different, surfaces such as glass, aluminum,
car, and airplane sheets. The coated surfaces were dried,
washed extensively with water, and finally polished. By this
procedure, transparent, thin coatings of about 400 nm
thickness were achieved that were invisible to the human
eye. These coatings exhibited highly enhanced contact an-
gles of the test liquids deposited on each surface, indicating
a significant reduction of the surface energies (Table 1).
The surface free energy of a solid can be used as a guide
to its relative adhesive properties. However, it is not a
straightforward exercise to measure the surface free energy
of solids directly. For practical reasons, procedures based
on contact angle measurements are regularly used.[10,16±20]
Dynamic contact angles (y) of test liquids with different
surface tensions were measured by means of the sessile
drop method (Table 1).[21] It is found, in particular, that all
non-polar liquids that spread readily on the pristine sur-
faces show high contact angles on complex-coated surfaces.
From the contact angles of a homologous series of non-
polar liquids the critical surface tension gc of a surface can
be determined as introduced by Zisman.[16] gc defines the
wettability of a solid by denoting the lowest surface tension
a liquid can have and still exhibit a contact angle greater
than zero degrees. The gc of a surface is usually obtained
from a Zisman plot of contact angles;[16] i.e., a plot of cos y
versus the surface tensions of non-polar liquids gl, with the
extrapolation of gl to cos y = 1 giving gc. Zisman plots result
in critical surface tensions varying from 13.1 mN/m to
5.7 mN/m depending on the coated material (Fig. 1 and
Table 2). The latter value is identical to the lowest critical
surface tension encountered to date,[22] which was obtained
for an oriented close-packed monolayer of perfluorododec-
anoic acid. It is widely accepted that such low critical
surface tensions characterize the resulting properties of a
surface of closed-packed perfluoromethyl CF3 groups.[8,23]
